summary:
• Middle East supply disruptions, including the Saudi refinery outage extending until 2027 and ongoing shipping concerns despite U.S.-Iran agreements, create near-term supply tightness that could support Western Hemisphere crude exports and pricing.

• Geopolitical uncertainties around Iran sanctions relief, Ukraine conflict trajectory, and Hormuz shipping routes remain unresolved despite recent diplomatic signals, leaving material downside risk to global oil balances and potential support for North American producers.

• A forecasted massive global oil surplus by 2027 from returning Middle East supply threatens long-term price support for Western Hemisphere producers, pressuring investment returns even as North American oil sands maintain low-cost competitive advantages.

articles.title: How the Oil Sands Became the Lowest-Cost North American Producer
articles.description: After the oil price crash of 2014-15, global energy majors like BP (NYSE:BP), Chevron (NYSE:CVX), and TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) sold their interests in the Canadian oil sands, at the time classifying their Canadian operations as among the most expensive and least profitable.
